title: A Better Estimate of Internally Generated Intangible Capital
abstract: Internally developed intangibles are not included in reported assets under U.S. generally accepted accounting principles. The omission of this increasingly important class of assets reduces the usefulness and relevance of financial statement analysis, conducted with reported values of equity and assets. Recent studies overcome this deficiency by capitalizing research and development (R&D) expenses to estimate the value of knowledge capital and by capitalizing selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses to estimate the value of organization capital. Those two estimates are then added to reported values for financial statement analysis. For convenience, many studies rely on two rules of thumb and assume them to be equally applicable in all instances: (1) 30% of SG&A and 100% of R&D expenses are investments, and (2) the useful life of SG&A and R&D investments is three and five years, respectively. We propose a more flexible approach by estimating the capitalization and amortization parameters on an industry–year–specific basis. Our modified values of total assets and equity, inclusive of the value of capitalized intangibles, exhibit greater association with future returns and investments compared with as-reported values and values estimated with uniform rules of thumb. We provide a better estimate of intangible capital for the consumers of financial statements.

author.Aneel Iqbal: Thunderbird School of Global Management, Arizona State University, Phoenix, Arizona 85004;
author.Shiva Rajgopal: Columbia Business School, Columbia University, New York, New York 10027;
author.Anup Srivastava: Haskayne School of Business, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta T2N 1N4, Canada
author.Rong Zhao: Haskayne School of Business, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta T2N 1N4, Canada

article.1. Introduction: Given the emergence of billion dollar–valued unicorns and trillion dollar–valued knowledge companies, stating that a firm’s in-house intangible investments, on average, create future benefits perhaps is uncontroversial.1However, U.S. generally accepted accounting principles (GAAP) require most such investments to be expensed as incurred. This is unlike expenditures on property, plant, and equipment (PP&E) and acquired intangibles that are capitalized and reported on balance sheets as assets. The omission of in-house intangibles, undoubtedly an important class of assets today, reduces the usefulness of financial statements and financial statement analysis, conducted with the book value of assets and equity (Rajgopal et al. 2023). For example, the calculations of the residual income valuation model and ratios such as Tobin’s Q, market-to-book, return on invested capital, asset turnover, debt-to-equity, and asset coverage, require a reliable estimate of the book value of assets and equity. Meanwhile, the importance of intangibles in the U.S. economy keeps increasing as each new cohort of public firms spends more on intangibles than its predecessor cohort (Corrado et al. 2005;Srivastava 2014,2023). Recent studies attempt to overcome the omission of in-house intangible capital in reported assets by capitalizing the outlays reported as selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) expenses (e.g.,Hulten and Hao 2008,Peters and Taylor 2017,Eisfeldt et al. 2022,Falato et al. 2022). Those studies typically rely on mechanical rules of thumb for convenience, such as treating a uniform 30% or 50% or even 100% of SG&A as investments irrespective of the firm’s industry affiliation, its business model, and the economic conditions prevalent during the measurement year. Compounding this invariant capitalization assumption (how much of research and development (R&D) and SG&A to capitalize) is another assumption: that the useful lives of those operating investments are uniform across all industries and time. Thus, two critical parameters used to estimate values of capitalized intangibles in a perpetual inventory model are assumed to be uniform across all companies and time. On top of that, by capitalizing 100% of R&D, prior studies do not differentiate between maintenance and investment R&D. Maintenance R&D refers to outlays that improve products and processes or simply support operations during the current period, not in a distant future. Not doing so is contrary to an accepted fact that fast-changing industries, such as telecommunications, antivirus software, and cybersecurity fields, spend a large part of R&D to at best maintain their current competitive position. Ignoring this fact also contradicts the tenet in International Accounting Standard (IAS) 38, the International Financial Reporting Standard (IFRS), which requires meeting stringent criteria before allowing capitalization of R&D expenditures. Importantly, IAS 38 requires firms to distinguish between investment and maintenance R&D. We propose a more flexible approach by letting the two critical parameters used for capitalization of intangibles to differ across industries and time. We argue that a uniform approach is inconsistent with intuition. For example, whereas SG&A includes investments that likely produce future benefits, it also covers maintenance expenditures that support existing operations. The percentage of maintenance expenses in SG&A (such as rents and sales discounts, commissions, and promotions) is likely higher in industries such as wholesale establishments and restaurants that need such outlays to support current business. In contrast, the percentage of investments in SG&A is likely higher in industries that aim to build long-term assets such as a distribution network, trademark, brand, or organizational capabilities to extract value from a patent or mining right. Furthermore, the useful lives of intangible capital built with SG&A investments differ by industry as they do for most PP&E investments. For example, the useful life of an advertising campaign for a sports event would be shorter than for a company that develops a new pharmaceutical brand protected with a newly approved patent. Similar arguments can be made for R&D. Companies with fast-changing technologies enjoy shorter benefits of R&D than those with slow, enduring technologies. We propose a new method to estimate the value of internally generated intangibles. We incorporate industry-specific capitalization and amortization rates for R&D and operational SG&A (SG&A minus R&D) outlays and let them vary over time.2Our method is based on identifying the best regression model that links current expenditures to a series of future economic benefits. Using a regression method to estimate parameters is not a new idea and is used in prior studies (e.g.,Lev and Sougiannis 1996,Banker et al. 2019). Those studies regress future operating earnings on lagged values of either SG&A or R&D. We make two modifications to these models. First, unlike prior studies, we use future revenues instead of operating income as the outcome variable. Our modification is based on the principle of matching because, at least based on the accounting conventions, expenditures are matched to revenues, not to profits. Moreover, operating profits are calculated after deducting investments included in R&D and SG&A. Second, we use outlays as the dependent variable (not as an independent variable as in prior studies) and employ current revenues and a series of future revenues as independent variables. This enables us to isolate the portion of expenditures associated with current or future revenues, controlling for the potential impact of such expenditures on other periods’ revenues. This calculation is not feasible if just a one-period-ahead future benefit is used as the dependent variable. We estimate two regressions, one each with R&D and operational SG&A as the dependent variable. In each case, right-hand-side variables are current revenues and a series of future revenues.3For R&D, we estimate eight regressions on an industry-year basis using none to a series of up to seven years of future revenues on the right-hand side. We assume that R&D yields future benefits for up to seven years in addition to the current period. For operational SG&A, we estimate six regressions on an industry-year basis, using none to a series of up to five years of future revenues. Operational SG&A is assumed to stay productive for up to five years. In both cases, we retain the equation on an industry-year basis that provides the highest adjustedR2values. That equation, which we call the optimal model, simultaneously identifies the investment portions and the useful lives of that intangible expenditure. The investment portion is part of the intangible expenditure that is associated with future revenues, and the useful life is the number of years of future revenues. We begin by documenting significant variation in capitalization and amortization rates across industries and time, casting doubt on the often-used assumption of uniform and invariant rates. For extractive industries such as metal mining and oil, the investment portions in operational SG&A exceed 80% because that number includes exploration and dry-hole expenses. For industries that are marketing-intensive or must keep adjusting products with shifting customer preferences (medical equipment, computers, business services, electronic equipment, wholesale, food products, and pharmaceuticals), the investment portion in operational SG&A is also higher (around 80%) than in other industries. In addition, the investment portion of R&D ranges from 40% to 90% for most industries; that is, it is not 100%. These findings cast doubt on a critical assumption in the prior studies that the investment portions of intangible expenditures are uniform across firms. The useful lives for R&D and operational SG&A also differ across industries, averaging 4.8 and 3.4 years, respectively. For example, R&D has an average life of 5.9 years for the electronic equipment industry and only 2.2 years for the rubber and plastic products industry. Operational SG&A has an average useful life of 3.1 years for the retail industry and 4.7 years for the business services industry (e.g., computer programming or R&D labs). This result contradicts the second main assumption used in prior studies: that the useful lives, thus the amortization schedules, of intangible investments are uniform across industries. More important, we find that the useful lives of operational SG&A are shorter than the five years considered in prior literature that assumes a 20% amortization rate (Peters and Taylor 2017,Falato et al. 2022). Not considering 100% of R&D as investments and incorporating a shorter life of operational SG&A lowers our estimates of capitalized intangible stock than estimations in prior studies. We also find that the investment portions and useful lives for both R&D and operational SG&A differ across decades (untabulated), indicating that they are not invariant across time even within industries. We then estimate the value of in-house intangible capital using industry–year–specific parameters and the perpetual inventory model. Parameters are estimated with a rolling sample of the past six years’ observations for operational SG&A and eight years’ observations for R&D. We do so to avoid look-ahead bias and to establish the out-of-sample validity of our estimates.4We add the computed value of in-house intangible capital stocks to reported book values of assets and equity to calculate what we call the modified values. Modified values of equity increase in all cases, but the increase varies across industries from less than 14% to as much as more than 220%. The market-to–modified book ratio thus calculated with new values falls relative to the as-reported values and converge closer to one on average. The modified ratio displays lower intraindustry variation (untabulated), which can be interpreted as a greater similarity in the growth prospects of industry firms after considering the role of intangibles.5The relative percentage contribution to an increase in book value, on average, is 15% and 85%, respectively, because of R&D and operational SG&A capitalization. The top industries with the highest percentage increase in book value are business services, pharmaceutical products, medical equipment, consumer goods, and candy and soda. However, the effects are not the same because of the two differing categories of intangibles. Business services, consumer goods, and candy and soda demonstrate the largest impact primarily because of operational SG&A capitalization. For pharmaceutical products and medical equipment, both R&D capitalization and operational SG&A capitalization are significant contributors to the increase in value of equity. We investigate the validity and economic implications of our method by conducting two tests that are of most interest to practitioners and academics. Our first test examines high minus low (HML) factor-based value investing, which uses the book-to-market (BTM) ratio to determine investment portfolios (Fama and French 1992,1993,2005;Lakonishok et al. 1994). HML is increasingly considered an important validation test for capitalized intangibles (Arnott et al. 2021,Choi et al. 2021,Li 2021,Lev and Srivastava 2022). HML returns based on our modified book-to-market ratio outperform returns based on as-reported numbers (by 2.4% per year on average) and uniform capitalization (by 1.4% per year on average). We conduct tests by dividing firms into two groups (large and small) according to the market value of equity (Lev and Srivastava 2022). We find stronger results for larger firms in terms of outperforming both as-reported HML and mechanically adjusted HML. We also conduct tests by decades and find that the outperformance is largely confined to the periods of 2001–2010 and 2011–2022, the periods when intangibles became a key factor of production and knowledge-intensive companies assumed greater importance in the economy relative to earlier decades. Results are stronger for intangible-intensive industries, such as business equipment, chemicals, and healthcare, consistent with expectations. Our second test followsPeters and Taylor (2017)and examines whether recalculated Tobin’s Q better predicts future investments (measured as the sum of capital expenditures and intangibles, inclusive of in-house intangible investments) relative to Tobin’s Q calculated with as-reported book values.6We also consider Tobin’s Q proposed byPeters and Taylor (2017), which has become a commonly used variable in empirical accounting, finance, and economics studies, indicating significant academic and practitioner demand for a more accurate Q. Our recalculated Tobin’s Q outperforms both thePeters and Taylor (2017)Tobin’s Q and the Tobin’s Q calculated with as-reported book values. Also, beating thePeters and Taylor (2017)Q, even by a small percentage, is noteworthy because the authors of that paper select the best model after trying all possible combinations and variations of capitalization percentages and useful lives. We contribute to the literature by providing a better estimate of the value of internally generated intangible capital. Our inductive approach likely would benefit those users of financial statements who currently estimate the values of internal intangibles via a mechanical approach. Demand is growing for improved estimates of capital employed in business because of the omission of an important class of assets from books, evident from numerous recent papers, particularly those that address the failure of factor-based value investing in recent times (Arnott et al. 2021,Choi et al. 2021,Li 2021,Eisfeldt et al. 2022,Lev and Srivastava 2022). Any method that improves the identification of expenditures that produce future benefits (and, thus, must be capitalized) and those that do not produce future benefits (and should not be capitalized) would arguably lead to a better value estimate than a mechanical rule of thumb. In addition, better estimates of intangible investments made by different industries and their useful lives should interest decision makers and policy experts, who believe in the maxim “If you can’t measure it, you can’t manage it.” The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.Section 2summarizes the prior literature and explains the measurement of intangible investments and the measurement of modified book value.Sections 3–6detail the empirical tests, andSection 7concludes.
article.2. Prior Literature and Proposal for a S: In this section, we discuss prior literature on the need for an improved measure of intangible capital, and propose a new method for the measurement of internally generated intangibles. 2.1. Prior Literature on Measuring the Value of Internally Generated Intangibles The outlays on internally generated intangibles are recognized in two major expense categories, R&D and operational SG&A. Earlier research mainly focuses on using capitalized R&D as a proxy for intangible capital (e.g.,Lev and Sougiannis 1996,Chan et al. 2001). More recent studies consider another type of intangible stock commonly referred to as organization capital, estimated by capitalizing operational SG&A investments (Peters and Taylor 2017,Banker et al. 2019).7Operational SG&A includes outlays on strategy, market research, brand creation and management, media management, customer acquisition, sales promotion, customer intelligence and relationship, supplier and partnership management, social networks, hiring and training expenses, management consulting, business process experiment and improvement, software development, and human capital (Enache and Srivastava 2018). Operational SG&A, thus, covers a wider variety of investments other than R&D that create intangible building blocks for the company, which might earn future revenues and profits (Eisfeldt and Papanikolaou 2014,Peters and Taylor 2017,Falato et al. 2022). Numerous academics, investment professionals, and policy makers have called for better recognition and disclosure of in-house intangibles (Srivastava and Rajgopal 2023).8Given that accounting rules on internally generated intangibles are under consideration and are likely to be so for a long time because of the lengthy comment period and accounting regulators’ prolonged drafting process, researchers rely on statistical models to estimate the value of intangibles. Studies follow the same method for accumulating intangible expenditures to estimate the capital stock of intangibles as is used for capitalizing PP&E investments. That is, investments are initially capitalized and then depreciated or amortized over their useful lives, using a perpetual inventory method. Two key inputs required to estimate this model are how much of the in-house intangible expenditures should be capitalized and over which period should those capitalized in-house intangibles be amortized. To measure the value of knowledge capital, the conventional practice is to capitalize 100% of R&D expenses and then reduce the value of R&D capital by 20% a year, which implicitly assumes a life of five years (Chan et al. 2001,Falato et al. 2022).9As far as organization capital is concerned, various heuristic capitalization and depreciation rates are used in prior literature. For example,Peters and Taylor (2017)capitalize 30% andEisfeldt and Papanikolaou (2013)andFalato et al. (2022)capitalize 100% of SG&A expenses.10Importantly, these studies assume a uniform amortization rate across all industries: 15% inEisfeldt and Papanikolaou (2013)andLi et al. (2018)and 20% inPeters and Taylor (2017)andFalato et al. (2022). These studies, thus, assume a useful life of five to seven years for SG&A, similar to R&D.11 2.2. Proposal for a Superior Method We propose to estimate the investment portions and productive lives of operational SG&A and R&D by industry and time in a systematic way, which should arguably help in appraising a better value of intangible capital. An intuitive explanation for incorporating industry-specific estimates is that business processes and business models are more likely to be similar within an industry than across industries, given the scrutiny conducted in determining a firm’s industry classification.Enache and Srivastava (2018)describe why investment portions of operational SG&A are likely to differ by industry. The novel idea of decomposing R&D into a maintenance and an investment component comes from recent academic and practitioner literature.12 Our proposed methodology improves upon the two key aspects related to the measure of internally generated intangibles: the portion of in-house intangible expenditure that should be capitalized (referred to as the investment portion) and the number of years over which capitalized intangibles should be amortized. We estimate those parameters using different combinations ofEquation (1), aiming to identify the equation that maximizes the association between current intangible outlays (either operational SG&A (OpSG&A) orR&D) and economic benefits (proxied by current and future revenues):13 Whereas the use of revenue as a measure of economic benefit is similar toEnache and Srivastava (2018), our model includes both current and future revenues, whereas they include only current revenues.Enache and Srivastava (2018)aim to identify the maintenance portion of operational SG&A and refer to the residual portion as investment based on the idea that firms spend rationally to produce benefits in current or future periods (Fisher 1930).Enache and Srivastava (2018)acknowledge the limitations of their measure in that their investment estimates could include wasteful expenditures that do not produce future benefits, such as marketing campaigns that failed or empire-building expenditures by managers. We overcome this shortcoming by explicitly tracing intangible expenditures to future revenues. We aim to identify the productive portion of outlays that brings future benefits and label it the investment portion. A downside to our model is that it requires a long time series of future revenues, which imposes two costs. First, our method suffers from survival bias. Second, our out-of-sample predictions can be made only after the last future revenue considered in the model arrives, creating a significant time gap between the estimation and the application of parameters. This delay could induce staleness of data. Our research design also requires switching of left- and right-side variables compared withLev and Sougiannis (1996)andBanker et al. (2019).14 InEquation (1), we include up to seven years of future revenues when R&D is the dependent variable and up to five years of future revenues when operational SG&A is the dependent variable. In both instances, we exclude observations with nonpositive values of dependent variables. The choice of five and seven years comes from prior studies, includingLi and Hall (2020).15 We scale operational SG&A, R&D, and revenues by the average of the beginning and the ending total assets for the year, calling the resulting variablesOpSG&A,R&D, andRevenues.16However, this scalar suffers from the limitation of noninclusion of intangible investments in total assets as restricted by the current GAAP. To address this limitation, we conduct our estimation using an iterative process. In the initial stage, we use as-reported total assets as the deflator inEquation (1). We then enhance total assets by the capitalized values of R&D and operational SG&A estimated using our method (inEquations (3)and (4), respectively). We use the enhanced total assets as a scalar inEquation (1)and reestimate the investment portions and useful lives, which we call the first iteration. We repeat this process, in the second iteration; that is, we use enhanced total assets from the first iteration to reestimateEquations (1), (3), and (4). We stop after two iterations as the parameters stabilize. Any additional iterations would impose computational costs and can affect the adoption of our method by the user community. We estimateEquation (1)by Fama and French 48 industry classification and year on a rolling basis to reduce the staleness of the estimates. That is, we use the past five and seven years, respectively, ofOpSG&AandR&Ddata instead of all past data. For each industry and year, we vary the number of years of future revenues (from zero to five forOpSG&Aand from zero to seven forR&D) and identify the model that gives the highest adjustedR2, calling it the optimal model. In additional tests, we use the Akaike information criterion (AIC) for selecting the optimal model, discussed inSection 6. We considerβ0× Revenuesas the maintenance portion and𝛼+∑𝑛𝑘=1𝛽𝑘×𝑅𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑢𝑒𝑠𝑘α+∑k=1nβk×Revenueskas the investment portion.17The number of years of future revenues in the optimal model denotes the useful life of capitalized R&D or operational SG&A, which, in turn, allows us to calculate the straight-line amortization rate as 1/useful life.18 We apply the industry–year parameters to revenues for firm–years included in each rolling regression sample to estimate investment portions ofR&DandOpSG&A(calculated as𝛼𝑖𝑛𝑑,𝑡+∑𝑛𝑘=1𝛽𝑘,𝑖𝑛𝑑,𝑡×𝑅𝑒𝑣𝑒𝑛𝑢𝑒𝑠𝑖,𝑡+𝑘αind,t+∑k=1nβk,ind,t×Revenuesi,t+k). We then estimate investment percentages by dividing investment portions by the total amounts, that is, investmentR&D/R&Dand investmentOpSG&A/OpSG&A, respectively, for each firm–year.19We calculate the average of each of those percentages for a given industry–year. The averages are labeledInvestment%Ind,R&DandInvestment%Ind,OpSG&A, respectively The useful lives are calledUsefulLifeInd,R&DandUsefulLifeInd,OpSG&A. These parameters are for an estimation sample that has at least five and seven years of future revenues for the operational SG&A and R&D models, respectively; positive values of operational SG&A and R&D; and the necessary minimum observations for an industry–year. Having calculated these parameters, we apply them out of the sample on future years to avoid the look-ahead bias.20We do so by calculatingInvestment_R&Di,tfor a given firm as the product ofInvestment%Ind,t,R&D, estimated on an industry–year basis using the rolling sample of past industry data and the firm’s own current R&D. Similarly,Investment_OpSG&Ai,tis the product ofInvestment%Ind,t,OpSG&Aestimated from past industry data and the firm’s own current operational SG&A. We define the value of internally generated intangible capital (Intan_Capi,tmod) as the sum of knowledge capital (Ki,t) and organization capital (Oi,t) inEquation (2), whereKi,tandOi,tare capital stocks of R&D and operational SG&A investments, calculated using the perpetual inventory method presented inEquations (3)and (4), respectively: The industry-year-specific rate of the the investment portions of R&D (Operational SG&A), δInd,t,R&D(δInd,t,OpSG&A), is calculated as 1 /UsefulLifeInd,t,R&D(1 /UsefulLifeInd,t,OpSG&A). Thus, the capitalized value at the end of the year equals the sum of last year’s value, which remains unamortized this year, and this year’s investments. For a company with no past data, the last year’s value is assumed to be zero. Finally, we estimate the modified book value (BVmodi,t) by adding the value of internally generated intangible capital (Intan_Capi,tmod) to the as-reported book value (BVi,t) inEquation (5):
article.3. Sample Selection and Empirical Result: Table 1summarizes our sample selection for estimating industry–year parameters. We start with 519,486 firm–year observations in Compustat between fiscal years 1970 and 2022. We exclude 197,584 observations with missing operational SG&A expenses, sales, or beginning- or end-of-the-year total assets. We use the Fama and French 48 industry classification and exclude 50,039 observations with missing Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) codes, financial and real estate firms (Fama and French industries 44, 45, 46, and 47), and firms in the “almost nothing” industry category (Fama and French industry 48), similar toEnache and Srivastava (2018). This yields a base sample of 271,863 observations. For the R&D regression, we exclude observations with nonpositive values for current year R&D and observations with missing future revenues for the next seven years. For the operational SG&A regression, we exclude observations with nonpositive values for current yearOpSG&Aand observations that have missing future revenues for the next five years. These exclusions result in an estimation sample of 166,128 and 62,399 firm–year observations, respectively, for operational SG&A and R&D regressions. The samples used in later tests vary because of additional data requirements. Table 1.Investment Portions and Productive Lives: Estimation Sample Table 1.Investment Portions and Productive Lives: Estimation Sample Notes.This table presents the selection procedure for the sample used in the estimation of the investment portions and useful lives of research and development (R&D) and operational SG&A (selling, general, and administrative expenses excluding R&D) during 1970–2022. The estimation process is described inSection 2. SeeAppendix Afor variable definitions. Table 2presents the average amounts of operational SG&A and R&D (scaled by average total assets) across Fama and French 48 industries and four decades from 1981 to 2022 (1981–1990, 1991–2000, 2001–2010, 2011–2022).21The table shows a steady increase over time in average intangible intensity, consistent withSrivastava (2014). The average operational SG&A increased from 29.0% of assets in the first decade (1981–1990) to 34.4% in the last decade (2011–2022). Similarly, the average R&D increased from the first decade (2.2% of assets) to the last decade (3.6% of assets). Consistent with intuition, innovation-intensive industries such as pharmaceutical products, medical equipment, computers, electronic equipment, and business services (software) have high R&D intensity. Industries such as candy and soda and retail have high operational SG&A intensity given their marketing-intensive businesses. Table 2.Average R&D and Operational SG&A by Industry and Decade Table 2.Average R&D and Operational SG&A by Industry and Decade Notes.This table reports the average values of R&D and operational SG&A (selling, general, and administrative expenses excluding R&D) as a percentage of total assets by Fama and French 48 industries (excluding finance firms and firms in the “almost nothing” category) and decade (1981–1990, 1991–2000, 2001–2010, 2011–2022). For ease of exposition, we include data from years 2021 and 2022 in the 2011–2022 decade. 3.1. Iterative Estimates of Parameter We first estimateEquation (1)using as-reported total assets as the scalar and then conduct two more iterations. Untabulated results show that the correlations of parameters from the initial-stage estimation to the first iteration are 0.85 for the investment portion of operational SG&A, 0.90 for the investment portion of R&D, 0.57 for the useful life of operational SG&A, and 0.73 for the useful life of R&D. We also find high correlations between the first and the second iterations (the investment portion of operational SG&A (0.92), the investment portion of R&D (0.97), the useful life of operational SG&A (0.65), and the useful life of R&D (0.88)). Henceforward, we use and discuss the estimates from the second iteration.22 Overall, results show that the investment portions of operational SG&A and R&D vary widely across industries (Table 3) and time (untabulated) and differ significantly from the 30% and 100% values, respectively, assumed in the prior literature.23Table 3shows that the highest proportion of investment operational SG&A is in medical equipment, pharmaceutical products, computers, business services, wholesale, electronic equipment, and extractive industries (such as mining, precious metals, and petroleum and natural gas) with each exceeding 80%. For these industries, the investment portion of operational SG&A likely represents brand building, marketing, customer acquisition, and development of organizational competence (except for extractive industries for which their exploration and dry-hole expenses are included in operational SG&A). The investment portions of R&D for pharmaceutical products, equipment (medical equipment, electronic equipment machinery, measuring and control equipment), and computers are among the highest at 91% and above, which is consistent with intuition. For most other industries, the investment portion of R&D ranges from 50% to 90%.Table 3also shows that the weighted average useful life for R&D across most industries is approximately five years.24 Table 3.Average Investment Portions and Useful Lives by Industry over 1981–2022 Table 3.Average Investment Portions and Useful Lives by Industry over 1981–2022 Notes.This table reports the estimation results on the investment portions and useful lives of research and development (R&D) and operational SG&A (selling, general, and administrative expenses excluding research and development,OpSGA). We use an iterative process in which as-reported assets are first used as a scalar inEquation (1)and the estimates of investment portions and useful lives are called initial-stage parameters. They are used to estimate the value of knowledge or organization capital, which is added to as-reported assets. The estimation process is described inSection 2. The enhanced assets are used as the scalar inEquation (1), and the revised estimates of investment portions and useful lives are called first iteration parameters. The process is repeated, using one more new calculation of enhanced assets, and the revised estimates are called second iteration parameters. This table presents the average investment portions and useful lives by Fama and French 48 industries (excluding finance firms and firms in the “almost nothing” category) over the 42-year study period, spanning 1981–2022. SeeAppendix Afor variable definitions. We observe significant variation in the useful life of R&D across industries despite restricting the productive life to seven years in the estimation model. Whereas a few industries (machinery and chemicals) have an average useful life of R&D exceeding six years, we find, on average, shorter lives ranging from two to five years for most other industries. Similarly, we find a wide variation of productive lives for operational SG&A from two to four and a half years with a weighted average of 3.4 years. This shorter life implies a significantly faster amortization rate than the 15%–20% considered in prior studies. 3.2. Application Sample We test whether our method offers superior estimates for investment portions and useful lives of intangibles. To avoid look-ahead bias, the parameters calculated using the rolling data until the previous year’s revenue (that is, the last future revenue in the estimation model) are used to estimate modified book values this year. We do not require the application sample to have minimum observations per industry year. Panel A ofTable 4shows the selection of the application sample from 1981 to 2022 used to test the validity of the modified book value (tests described inSection 4).25We obtain a larger sample of 143,048 firm–year observations, representing 14,735 distinct firms, than for the estimation samples. Panel B presents descriptive statistics of application firms. The mean (median) as-reported book value of equity is $1,141 million ($99 million). After capitalization of internally generated intangibles, the average modified book value increases to $1,825 million ($181 million). The average increase over the reported book value is 131%. This increase is attributable to an average of $133 million because of R&D capitalization (that is, knowledge capital) and an average of $526 million because of operational SG&A capitalization (that is, organization capital). The relative contribution of organization capital is 82%, much larger than the relative contribution of 18% because of knowledge capital. Panel C shows that the average modified book value per share is $16.7 compared with the average as-reported book value per share of $9.6. Of this increase, $6.1 is because of organization capital and $0.9 is because of knowledge capital. Table 4.Impact of Including Values of In-House Intangibles on Book Values of Equity Table 4.Impact of Including Values of In-House Intangibles on Book Values of Equity Notes.This table shows the impact of capitalizing internally generated intangibles on the book value of equity. The value of internally generated intangibles is the sum of knowledge capital and organization capital estimated using the perpetual inventory method. Knowledge capital is the remaining value of the capitalized research and development (R&D). Organization capital is the remaining value of the capitalized operational SG&A (selling, general, and administrative expenses excluding XRD,OpSGA)). The estimation process is described inSection 2. Panel A presents the sample selection criteria. Panel B (panel C) presents the descriptive statistics for the full sample (on a per-share basis). Panel D reports the average impact of knowledge capital and organization capital on book value by Fama and French 48 industries (excluding finance firms and firms in the “almost nothing” category). SeeAppendix Afor variable definitions. Panel D ofTable 4shows the average percentage increase in book value because of intangible capitalization as well as the relative contributions of organization versus knowledge capital by Fama and French 48 industries. The top industries with the highest percentage increase in book value are business services (220.2%), pharmaceutical products (199.6%), medical equipment (195.8%), consumer goods (194.1%), and candy and soda (190.2%). On a relative basis, organization capital is the primary contributor of the book value increase for business services, consumer goods, and candy and soda. For pharmaceutical products and medical equipment, both knowledge capital and organization capital are significant contributors to the book value increase. The increase in book value because of intangible capitalization is substantially smaller for many old-economy industries, such as mining, precious metals, utilities, and business supplies.
article.4. HML Factor–Based Investing: HML methodology, widely used in the finance literature and value-investing practices, is based on the idea that the book-to-market ratio is a proxy for undervaluation. High BTM indicates that stock prices are depressed relative to the underlying value (proxied by book value). Hence, buying high-BTM stocks, called value stocks, should yield above-market returns when stock prices recover to underlying values. Similarly, low-BTM stocks, called glamour stocks, should be overvalued and fall in price over time. Shorting the overvalued stocks should yield positive returns over time when their prices get corrected. HML returns are based on buying high-BTM and shorting low-BTM stocks (Fama and French 1992,1993;Lakonishok et al. 1994). Whereas the HML strategy has yielded positive returns since the 1960s, HML returns, as reported on Kenneth French’s website, have been negative in recent years, on average, particularly in the 21st century (Lev and Srivastava 2022).26We examine whether HML factor–based investing can be improved with our modified book-to-market ratio. We call the recalculated returns modified HML returns. In addition to our method, we calculate returns from BTM with uniform capitalization and amortization rates across industries for benchmarking purposes. We calculate adjusted book values by capitalizing 30% (100%) of operational SG&A (R&D) and amortizing operational SG&A (R&D) investment over the useful life of three years (five years) to maintain parity with the prior literature. We call the recalculated returns mechanically adjusted HML returns. 4.1. Modified HML Returns Relative to As-Reported HML Returns We followFama and French (1992,1993) to calculate modified HML returns based on modified book value. We make one modification by using within-industry ranks of book-to-market ratio for determining cutoffs for portfolio formation instead of ranks across all firms in a given year. This is consistent withAsness et al. (2000), who show that industry-based ranks lead to better returns. The calculation of HML returns is described inAppendix B. Our proposed modification of book values could affect HML returns in two ways (Lev and Srivastava 2022). First is by changing the feasible set of companies that enter a long or short portfolio because only firms with positive modified book values are considered for inclusion in long and short portfolios. Numerous firms whose book values turn positive with the addition of intangible capital stock now qualify for inclusion in portfolios. Second is by changing the relative ranks based on the BTM ratio, thereby altering the chance of whether a company enters a long or short portfolio. Related to these two points, the BTM cutoffs for calculating long and short portfolios also change. Panel A ofTable 5(andFigure 1(a)) presents the excess of annual modified HML returns over as-reported HML returns by decade for the 42 years from 1981 to 2022. Positive (negative) excess returns indicate that the modified HML outperforms (underperforms) as-reported HML returns. This test is about beating as-reported HML and not about beating the market on a risk-adjusted basis. Our purpose is not to test the validity of the HML method but, instead, to test whether we can improve on the HML method.Lev and Srivastava (2022)show that the impact of intangible capitalization on HML returns differs by size. Hence, we also tabulate excess HML returns for small and large stocks separately. Table 5.HML Factor-Based Investing Table 5.HML Factor-Based Investing Notes.This table shows the impact of intangible capitalization on high minus low returns over the period from 1981 to 2022. Panel A shows the excess of the annual average of modified HML returns over as-reported HML returns, by decade.t-statistics are for pairedt-tests using industry aggregate market value at the time of portfolio formation as weights. Panel A also shows excess returns over the mechanically adjusted HML returns. Positive (negative) excess returns indicate the outperformance (underperformance) of modified HML. The panel also presents the same values for big and small firm categories. Panel B reports the average percentage changes in firms’ book-to-market ratios along with the number of years in which excess returns are positive. Panel C shows the excess returns by Fama and French 12 industry sectors (excluding finance and utilities). Panel D presents the number of years in each decade when excess returns are positive, by industries. For ease of exposition, we include data from years 2021 and 2022 in the 2011–2022 decade. *, **, and *** represent the significance of excess returns at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively, in two-tailedt-tests. Panel A shows no significant improvement over as-reported HML during the 1980s and 1990s, arguably because the intangibles had still not assumed much importance in the economy and our method makes book values noisier. However, during the decades 2001–2010 and 2011–2022, modified HML outperforms as-reported HML. The weighted average annual excess HML returns (modified HML returns minus as-reported HML returns) is 4.6% during 2001–2010 and 2.6% during 2011–2022 compared with 0.2% during 1981–1990 and −1.8% during 1991–2000.27 Panel B shows that modified HML returns outperform as-reported HML returns in 6 of the 10 years during 1981–1990, 7 years during 1991–2000, 8 years during 2001–2010, and 8 years during 2011–2022. This pattern coincides with the magnitude of changes in BTM because of intangible capitalization. During 1981–1990 and 1991–2000, the average percentage increase in individual firms’ BTM determined using our method (compared with using as-reported book value) is 121.6% and 123.3%, respectively. This difference becomes more pronounced during the recent decades, rising to 136.4% during 2001–2010 and 143.8% during 2011–2022. Panel A ofTable 5also presents results by decades after dividing the sample by size. The large firm portfolio shows greater outperformance of modified HML than the small firm portfolio.28Over the 42-year period, the averages of excess HML returns are 2.4%, 2.9%, and 2.1% for the full sample, large firms, and small firms, respectively. Tests show that the excess returns are significantly different from zero with pairedt-statistics (calculated by weighting observations by industry aggregate market value at the time of portfolio formation) of 4.14, 3.55, and 3.48, respectively. Panel C ofTable 5(andFigure 1, (b)–(d)) reports the annual average of excess HML returns by the 12 Fama and French industry sectors (excluding finance and utilities) for our sample period of 1981 to 2022. Results show that the modified HML outperforms as-reported HML across eight industry sectors (except the telecom and shops sectors) with the highest average excess HML returns of 8.2% for chemicals, 4.5% for healthcare, and 4.0% for others. (Others include mines, construction, construction materials, transportation, hotels, business services, and entertainment.) Untabulated results show that modified HML returns outperform as-reported HML returns in 29 out of 40 sector-decades (10 sectors × 4 decades). For the large firm category, modified HML outperforms as-reported HML for all sectors except the telecom and consumer durables sectors. For the small firm sample, modified HML outperforms as-reported HML for all sectors except the shops sector. 4.2. Modified HML Returns Relative to Mechanically Adjusted HML Returns Our method outperforms as-reported HML, indicating the validity of intangible capitalization at least for value-investing purposes. However, tests inSection 4.1do not establish the superiority of our method compared with mechanical capitalization. The question is as follows: should an investor impose data collection and computational costs to calculate the capitalization parameters by industry and year or simply assume them to be uniform across industries and time? We examine this question in panel A ofTable 5(andFigure 2), which shows that modified HML also outperforms mechanically adjusted HML on average. The weighted average annual excess HML returns (modified HML returns minus mechanically adjusted HML returns) is 1.4% for the full sample (significantly different from zero at ap-level of 1%). Examining by decade, we find that modified HML outperforms during the recent decades of 2001–2010 (2.4%) and 2011–2022 (2.4%) but underperforms mechanically adjusted HML during the 1990s (−4.4%). During the most recent period of 2011–2022, modified HML outperforms mechanically adjusted in 8 out of 12 years (Table 5, panel B). Panel A also shows the results after dividing the sample by size. The outperformance of modified HML is higher for large firms during the most recent decade of 2011–2022 (3.1%) than for small firms (1.9%). Panel C shows that, for large firms, modified HML outperforms mechanically adjusted HML for all industry sectors except for consumer durables (−0.6%), energy (−0.6%), and telecom (−4.3%). For small firms, modified HML outperforms mechanically adjusted HML for all industries except for business equipment (−1.0%) and shops (−0.5%). Panel D ofTable 5shows the numbers of years in each decade, by industry, in which modified HML return outperforms as-reported and mechanically adjusted HML returns. The outperformance of modified HML returns is concentrated in the last two decades for most industries. On average, modified HML outperforms as-reported HML in 8 (out of 10) years and 8 (out of 12) years in the decades of 2001–2010 and 2011–2022, respectively. Furthermore, on average, modified HML outperforms mechanically adjusted HML in 9 (out of 10) years and 8 (out of 12) years in the decades of 2001–2010 and 2011–2022, respectively. Overall, our results indicate that investors are better off considering variation in capitalization parameters on an industry–year basis for their factor-based value investing than assuming them to be uniform across industries and time.
article.5. Explanatory Power for Investment-Q Re: Peters and Taylor (2017)establish the superiority of their measure compared with as-reported book value by testing whether Tobin’s Q, modified with mechanically adjusted book values, better predicts operating investments than Tobin’s Q based on reported numbers. Fazzari et al. (1988)andErickson and Whited (2012)measure Tobin’s Q as The main innovation introduced byPeters and Taylor (2017)is the inclusion of intangible capital in the denominator. They measure intangible capital as the sum of intangible assets recognized on the balance sheet (Intan) and internally generated intangible assets (Intan_CapPT) with the latter variable calculated by capitalizing 100% of R&D and 30% of operational SG&A. To assess whetherIntan_Capmodis a better measure of the unrecognized intangible capital of a firm, we recalculate Tobin’s Q by replacingIntan_CapPTwith our measure ofIntan_Capmodas previously defined and label thatqmod(modified Tobin’s Q): FollowingPeters and Taylor (2017), we compare the within-firmR2of the regression Table 6presents the results of this analysis. Panel A shows the sample selection of 119,459 firm–year observations. Panel B shows that the mean (median) of Tobin’s Q (q) based on as-reported numbers is 6.02 (1.60). The mean Tobin’s Q using thePeters and Taylor (2017)method (qPT) is 1.55, much lower thanq, because of a significant increase in book value with the inclusion of mechanically capitalized intangibles. The median goes below 1 to 0.80. The distribution of modified Tobin’s Q based on our method (qmod) is similar to that ofqPTwith mean (median) at 1.48 (0.79). However, our modified Tobin’s Q has a smaller standard deviation (2.26 forqmodcompared with 2.63 forqPT). Table 6.Improvement in the Association Between Investments and Tobin’s Q Table 6.Improvement in the Association Between Investments and Tobin’s Q Notes.This table examines whether a modified Tobin’s Q, including capitalization of in-house intangibles, based on two different capitalization methods, better explains future investments. We test this idea by comparing the within-firmR2of the ordinary least squares panel regressions of one-year-ahead investments (Investment) on three measures of Tobin’s Q.Investmentrepresents the flow value of total investment for the year (the sum of physical investment, recognized intangibles, and capitalized in-house intangibles) scaled by the stock value of total investment. Standard Tobin’s Q (q) is based on reported numbers measured as the firm’s market value divided by the replacement cost of physical capital. ThePeters and Taylor (2017)Tobin’s Q (qPT) is based on the capitalization of intangibles assuming uniform investment portions and uniform lives of intangibles across industries. Modified Tobin’s Q (qmod) is based on our method using industry–year–specific investment portions and amortization rates as described inSection 2. Panel A presents the sample selection for this analysis. Panel B reports descriptive statistics ofq,qPT, andqmod. Panel C presents the regression results for the full sample. Panel D shows the improvement in the explanatory power by decade. For ease of exposition, we include data from years 2021 and 2022 in the 2011–2022 decade. Thet-statistics are in parentheses. SeeAppendix Afor variable definitions. *, **, and *** represent the significance of differences at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively, in two-tailedt-tests. Panel C ofTable 6compares the explanatory power (within-firmR2) of the regressions for the three Tobin’s Q measures. Within-firmR2measured withqPT(30.4%) is higher than that withq(19.0%). Our modified measure (qmod) increases it further to 32.7%, an improvement of 13.7% and 2.3% over those based onqandqPT, respectively. Examining the results by decade in panel D, we find thatqmodoutperformsqandqPTin each of the four decades over the past 42 years. Tobin’s Q proposed byPeters and Taylor (2017)has become one of the most used measures in empirical accounting, finance, and economics studies. In that context, we show that a measure incorporating industry-specific capitalization and amortization rates improves the measurement of Tobin’s Q compared with the one based on uniform capitalization rates. Any outperformance is noteworthy.Peters and Taylor (2017)try different combinations of capitalization and amortization rates and select rates that give the best result (within-firmR2inEquation (9)). So beating that best result, even by a small margin, amounts to beating all other combinations.
article.6. Additional Analyses and Robustness Te: We summarize the additional analyses and robustness checks in this section. 6.1. Validation Test Using Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) We estimate the investment portion of R&D and operational SG&A, using the method described inSection 2. We now apply the same method to estimate the investment portion of the COGS. Results (untabulated) show that the average investment portion of COGS across Fama and French 48 industries is only 2.8% with 28 industries having an investment portion of less than 1%. This result is consistent with intuition because manufacturing costs are accumulated in the asset accounts as inventory and later expensed through COGS when the corresponding goods are sold. For service companies, only those outlays that can be traced to revenues are included in the cost of sales account. Thus, COGS likely represents what we call maintenance expenditures, not investments. 6.2. Using AIC for Optimal Model Selection Maximizing adjustedR2is just one among several statistical techniques used for selecting the optimal model. Alternative techniques include forward selection, backward elimination, stepwise, and information criterion–based model selection methods, all of which have their own advantages and disadvantages (Hocking 1976). We conduct an additional analysis using AIC as an alternative model selection criterion in our estimation of investment portions and productive lives of R&D and operational SG&A. Untabulated results show that the correlation between the value of intangible capital estimated under the two selection criteria is 0.85, suggesting that using AIC for model selection is unlikely to change our inferences significantly. 6.3. Longer Payoff Period for R&D in the Pharmaceutical Industry One limitation of our method is that the useful life of R&D is capped at seven years. For some industries, such as pharmaceuticals, the benefits from R&D outlays can extend well beyond that time frame. We conduct an additional analysis to address this limitation for the pharmaceutical industry (Fama and French industry 13). We include up to 15 years of future revenues inEquation (1)to estimate the investment portion and useful life of R&D. Untabulated results show that the average investment portion of R&D is 91%, the same as the investment portion of 91% inTable 3. The values of knowledge capital calculated using the revised and the original set of parameters are highly correlated (Pearson correlation of 0.99).30
article.7. Conclusion: An important class of assets (that is, in-house intangibles) is omitted from the balance sheets, which adversely affects the usefulness of financial ratios calculated with book values of assets and equity. With the rising importance of intangibles in firms’ business models, users of financial statements are increasingly interested in methods that can improve the estimates of book values. Numerous recent studies improve estimates of book values by using mechanical rules of thumb, such as the capitalization of 30% of operational SG&A and 100% of R&D, and by assuming five- and seven-year lives of those investments, respectively. We propose a new inductive method to estimate the capitalization and amortization rates for in-house intangible investments. We estimate the investment portions of R&D and operational SG&A and their useful lives simultaneously, using a regression approach. We allow these parameter estimates to vary by industry and time, thereby accommodating the heterogeneity in characteristics and productive lives of these intangible expenditures. Our approach leads to a superior estimate of intangible capital by better identification of expenditures that produce future benefits and those that turn out to be unproductive or produce just current period benefits. Ideally, from a financial statement user perspective, the former should be capitalized and included in the assets, but the latter should not be. We show that our modified book value improves factor-based investing (HML factor) relative to as-reported book value and the model based on mechanical capitalization. Results are stronger in the recent decades (2001–2010 and 2011–2022) and for intangible-intensive industries. Our modified book value also exhibits a greater association with future investments compared with the mechanically adjusted book value as perPeters and Taylor (2017). Our study contributes to the literature by providing a better estimate of book value that can be used by consumers of financial statements, particularly those who rely on some mechanical rule of thumb to modify book values. Because our analyses focus exclusively on the balance sheet side of intangible assets, future research could investigate the welfare implications of capitalization of intangibles for inclusion in reported assets.
